title: 2025/12/01 - PP (Part 3)
displayTitle: 2025-12-01 - Primary Production Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries [Part 3]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries
description: Monday 1 December 2025 | Primary Production Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries Hearing of evidence: Andrew Hoggard, Minister for Biosecurity, Minister for Food Safety, and Associate Minister for Agriculture (Animal Welfare)
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries. Andrew Hoggard, Minister for Biosecurity, Minister for Food Safety, and Associate Minister for Agriculture (Animal Welfare)

chunks.text: An hour,

Tony. Right. Um, just about all in the room. Thanks very much for, um, your attendance today, minister and, um, officials. So, um, we've got you until five 30. Um, we'll leave it in your capable hands. Okay. Well, thank you for finding me along.

Uh, I guess I would just probably start with, um, uh, I don't know if how many of you have seen the, um, press release, but, uh, today we announced the, um, what would you call it, moving to an confidence of absence around mycoplasma bovis. So pretty important milestone in, um, the fight against that disease.

It's been a lot of hard work from, uh, not only ministry, but the industry partners, but most importantly, the farmers themselves, hell of a lot of them. And Mark, you know, this only too well, uh, went through a hell of a lot of pain and anguish, um, with what had to happen on their farms.

And that's a massive burden that, um, those farmers sort of bore for the entire country. I know, um, my involvement with the International Dairy Federation, I was hearing from overseas as to the impacts that that disease had on a lot of other countries and their farming systems and how much of a challenge it was proving to a lot of them, uh, having to live with that disease.

And so, you know, I think this is a really, you know, we've done something no other country could do in terms of, um, getting to this point. And so hopefully in the, you know, we're on track by 2028 to have eliminated it. Um, I guess also in biosecurity with mentioning we had the fruit flying incursion earlier, or was it about this time last year?

Um, two of them. And they were handled very successfully. Uh, obviously we've got the hornet, I keep wanting to say wasp, but no, the hornets, um, uh, happening right now. And that's obviously ramping up to be a reasonably sizable campaign and our efforts to eliminate them and remove them.

And I think also with the HPAI, um, we've obviously had the program down in Dunedin over this last year to, uh, remove that from that farm that was successful. And then the operational agreement with industry, um, on how we manage that and being prepared for that.

That's, uh, quite important I feel. Uh, on terms of operational agreements, we obviously had the one around foot and mouth that was also announced. Um, in terms of food safety, I guess the key things I would, uh, point to in that regard is just generally a number of areas we were looking at, um, I'd say is removing red tape.

Um, trying to streamline the process and system, uh, to, you know, ensure that we can not add to the cost of food. Um, what do we need to do to ensure that food's safe and looking for ways of being smarter and more efficient in all aspects of how we do that. And we've got a food act improvement plan that's underway.

Now, obviously the announcement around cake makers was sort of the first deliverable on that, and there's more to come. Um, and obviously a CVM, um, that continues, you know, for a number of industry, they're concerned around the timeframe it's taking to get products into the country.

Recently signed an MOU with Australia to try and harmonize our system much more with Australia so that, um, in future, if you register in one country, then the other country will accept all of the work that was done by that other country and only require, you know, what may be relevant, uh, to New Zealand and our unique environment.

Uh, animal welfare, obviously, I'm sure you'll want to talk to me about, um, pig regulations. Um, so I guess keep the points there. I would make, um, this is about making sure we can have them in the faring crates for as little time as possible to save as many piglets as possible.

And that is the sole focus around that of, um, achieving that balance, which is the key thing you're after in farming is balancing everything up. You can't be siloed in your thinking. Uh, I think with that, I will leave it to you guys. Okay. So what we're gonna do is focus on biosecurity first.

Yep. Animal welfare, and then finally food safety. So for the last, the two, um, caution of it. So Joe's got the first question. Thank you. Um, nice to see you minister. Thanks for coming along and thanks for the team as well. Coming along. I want to ask you specifically about kpa Yep. And the hornet, but I'll start with Kpa, if that's all right.

Yep. So there was a report done earlier in the year that said it was going to, uh, cost $9.4 billion right. To our economy. You disputed that saying you weren't quite sure. You know, it could be debatable on the numbers. Um, but what are you seeing the impact of Kapa to our communities in New Zealand is, and if you like, I can rattle through my other three quick questions to go alongside if you want. Or do you wanna do them One at a time? I'll do 'em one at a time. So in terms of the impact, obviously we saw with the cyclone there was a big impact in

terms of, for those communities where it all washed up on the beach, big rotting stinking mess. Mm-hmm. Um, but in terms of the, the science of what we're starting to understand is that yes, I stick by that original statement that perhaps it may not be that much of an impact.

Um, the, we've had quite a significant dieback of it in a number of areas. And um, also there's been some al work done looking at actually what is the impacts on marine ecosystems. Um, and I think, uh, team can correct me if I'm wrong, but from my recall of that work, it was where you've got the solid mats, yes, there may be, um, some impact, but where it's sparse, um, actually I think I read that, um, you actually get a bit more of a environment for, um, microinvertebrates

and other things, and maybe the impact isn't so high. Our goal is still to, uh, reduce and eliminate. Um, but it's, you know, I guess where we're at the moment, we've launched the strategy on the weekend, uh, where we're sort of focused on that education, that control. And I think that's gonna be the key part of it, is making sure we can reduce down that spread, particularly when we've got, had this dieback occur.

Um, you know, uh, having everyone aware of, you know, when you drop an anchor, if you pick something up, then bag it, bin it, uh, and prevent that spread. And so that's probably gonna be a, one of our key things is that education, that awareness, uh, utilizing the controlled, um, area notices we've had, the, the research that's been done has, uh, so there's the UV C treatment and the chlorine tub of both been reasonably successful.

Obviously we think we can perhaps refine those a bit more and get the cost down. So if we do need to do, um, removal, then those are tools that are available to us. Uh, the rover, uh, we are sort of, I guess that had had been a bit of a slower burn project.

There was getting parts and stuff from Scotland, believe that's underway. Our challenge now is, um, well, there may not be enough RA to test it on, um, where it's, um, based. Um, so yeah, the, the mats and up in, um, the bay in the Bay of Islands where this was gonna be the test bed, um, they've had a dieback of, uh, up to 9200% wasn't.

Yeah. Yeah. So that might be a bit of a challenge in terms of completing that research because the, the problem ain't there to fix. So supplementary. Yeah. Um, thank you Mr. Chair. So, uh, on the back of that response, you know, the dieback, are you sure it's not just going to be a seasonal type dieback, a small, like, like there was that that other time, but also, um, you mentioned there, you know, about there not really being much of an impact for community, but have you done a cultural analysis of that given that, um, for Hapu

and Iwo Iwi, um, Ana as a, as a traditional source of, of food for them? So have you done any cultural analysis on the impact of Kapa for them? And sorry, what was the first part of that? Was the, Was around the eight, this is the dieback. Do you know if it's Small? Yeah, yeah. So I mean, we dunno, and that's part of what we'll be researching more with niwa in terms of truly understanding what's going on there and having a clear understanding as to is this just a one-off event due to mm-hmm.

Freak na, uh, nature event, or is this something we can expect more of? Is this something we can utilize actually in terms of removal, uh, in terms of cultural impact, the cultural impact around fish stocks? Like I said, the, the data or the science from niwa seemed to say that perhaps it wasn't as big as had been estimated.

Um, and I mean, I think John can probably talk to you at some point if you want, around, um, what that sort of science is showing. But, you know, I don't, I stick by the fact, I don't think it's this huge multi-billion dollar impact, um, at this stage.

It's not what we're seeing from. But you haven't done a cultural impact analysis? Uh, well, we've done lots of research with niwa, um, in terms of, we've heard from the community, we've, you know, you've had iwi, you've been involved with the steering group and fed into that, and so they're fed into the strategy. So my final suck then is do you agree with the assessment of Nicole and McDonald, see of the settlement trust who said government isn't taking it a serious as they need to, and if not, why not?

Well, Nicola has been part of, she was chair of our, she was strategy group. She, uh, put forth the strategy to us. I think, uh, we have paid a lot of attention to this. Um, so, you know, we've invested in the last two years, $15 million or about there, um, into this in terms of finding solutions to remove it.

So I think that given the tight fiscal times, we find ourselves in, it's, um, a substantial investment in it, Mr. Chair. Right. Um, gonna move on to Mark. Thank you, Mr. Chair. And sorry everyone for my absence. Um, I am in isolation waiting for a transplant of all things.

Um, minister, thank you so much for your time today. Biosecurity is obviously a huge issue. The Embo announcement is fantastic, and full disclosure, as you noted, I have it on farm. And this is perhaps a question as much for you, sir, as, as, as John sitting behind you, T 29, we, we acknowledge that maybe the vector was semen amongst other things.

The question I really have is, now that we've learned through this program and all the, all, all the difficulties the program presented to farmers, if T 29 was potentially semen, why wasn't T 21? Uh, that's more of a science question. I, I don't think anyone ever said it wasn't. Yeah, Yeah.

So, so could you say, say that for the record so I can hear that. Sorry. So Grace said he didn't think anyone had actually said that T 21 wasn't, um, the result of semen. I think from what I've seen, um, most of the evidence now is pointing, pointing to the fact that it was, uh, imported semen.

That was the victor that, uh, caused this. Yes. Thank you, minister. And do, do you think in the early parts of the program, the, obviously we hadn't had the learnings that we now have that mistakes were made in terms of how the program was officiated, given the mental illness, wellbeing issues that were often part of, you know, hurt eradication, do you think?

Uh, well, obviously I can't speak to that as minister, uh, because I wasn't a minister at that point in time. Yes. Um, but I was obviously involved in the periphery through federated farmers. And I think, you know, if you look at changes we're making to the biosecurity Act, we've put in a number of learnings from the M Bovis program into the changes to the biosecurity act, like having the, um, uh, disputes panel as being formalized within the biosecurity act.

Obviously early on there was, um, you know, farmers didn't have the same point of contact. Um, and also there wasn't that support for them. Uh, you know, I heard many stories of people who had ring up one week and then the next week they'd call the same number, but it was a different person they were talking to.

And that did cause a lot of stress. And I think those lessons have been well and truly learn going forward. Thank you, minister. Right. Uh, next is Sue. Um, hi. Yeah. Thank you for being here today. Uh, you mentioned the bird flow or the HPAI. Oh, yeah, yeah.

And I was just wanting to ask you about, I know that that's had a, the fact that that was, I mean, I think there're only two farms. One farm affected one. Yeah. But there's been a long term, long tail of businesses that have been affected because they can't export products to China. Yeah. Still. And, um, and I think the canned products, and they're accepted, but I'm thinking about, in particular, a large pet food company in my electric where because it's an extruded product, it still can't be used.

And although the rules are gonna change to allow heat treated stuff like these, it's gonna happen. But when is that gonna be, when is that gonna go? Sure. I, I, I just got back from China on Saturday and have signed the agreement that, uh, allows for the heat treated, um, uh, transfer of pet food. So that frees up about $35 million worth of export earnings back into The Fantastic. So, So that's done For those guys.

When can they put them on the ship and start sending them over? What does that mean, Steven? Uh, I dunno the exact date, but, but essentially, um, This year or next year? Absolutely. What, uh, well, it's December. Um, the Chinese, the head Chinese CU China customs and I, at my level, have signed the agreement Brilliant.

To allow for what the transfer of pet food on, I don't know if there's any further export. What they'll have to do is they'll, they'll have to publish an overseas market access requirements update, um, so that all companies can get on and, and do that. So I, I imagine that'll happen this month. There's nothing standing in the way of it. Yeah.

And I think, um, key thing here is obviously once you lose a country of free or freedom, country of freedom, by the way, around status in terms of one of these diseases, then you obviously can't export if that is a requirement from where you're exporting to. And, you know, there was nothing any of us could have done because the disease came here, we had it.

Um, and I think, so part of the thinking we've got to do going forward is do we want to go through the long process of reapplying for that country of freedom status when it could very well happen again, like it did in Australia. They'd gone through the process to reget it, and then, oh, it would've been only been a week or so later, um, they had it pop up again.

And so I think part of our thinking has been around how do we, you know, have agreements with other countries, um, around, you know, what are the proce like, like ray's done here? What are the processes to enable export to still continue if we don't have that country of freedom status?

Because as we all know this, it's slowly, thankfully slower than we might've worried moving around Antarctica. Um, I believe it's, I'm pretty sure it's, uh, got to herd island, um, out there in the South Indian Ocean. And so it probably is, you know, the potential there is, you know, it's a, a couple of years away that it might hit here.

And so the focus needs to be on how can we keep operating as a country if this disease gets here because it will become endemic. So those are the things we need to be working, working on. And this is what, what you've signed is gonna prevent. It's Been a really good, should there be future outbreaks? Well, well, well, because we've changed the standard.

So it's been a really good achievement by my people and the, with the Chinese colleagues to get to a point where we agree to change to the market access requirements and to try, so it's now only raw product that can't Yeah. Can't go to an ingredient. So, as I said, it's worth about 35 million to New Zealand food industry. So it's, it's a good move. Yeah. Oh, thank you. That's awesome. Great news. Well done.

Right, Steve, last question on biosecurity. The added of biosecurity should surely be extension time saves nine. Yes, correct. Repair was another good one. Your government has cut. Uh, this established 108 roles in biosecurity, which is the biggest cut in all of the NPI jobs lost.

We have no shortage of major issues with longstanding incursions, such as widening pines and new incursions, such as the yellow leg hornet. And whilst your government has, in a sense been the beneficiary of decisive action around mycoplasma bovis by the previous government, and we're all glad that that's been dealt with, is there a risk that your legacy minister will be that will see wilding pines getting worse, Madagascar rag wart getting worse and yellow leaked hornets taking hold here because you've actually cut the biosecurity defense net

that we need in this country? Well, I dispute that, and I think number of you have sent in, um, W PQS that I've seen recently asking what those job numbers were. And I'm, I can't remember the number off the top of my head, but correct me if I'm wrong, the frontline staff and biosecurity had actually gone up. So there's more people focused on the frontline than they had previously been.

Yes, there was some back office roles that were disestablished. Um, but there is more of a focus now on the frontline. I think, you know, in terms of hornets, we are working really effectively. We have gotten in, uh, experts from around the world to give us advice and tell us their view on what needs to happen.

We are listening to that advice and making decisions based on that advice. As you know, the situation changes. So look, I look at a number of things we've done, um, and I think actually we're moving forward on this. You know, it's, yeah, the operational agreement around foot and mouth had been languishing for about a decade or more.

Uh, I gave a clear direction that I wanted that sorted. We got sorted, um, you know, fruit flight, we sorted that out. Avian flu, we've sorted that out. So I think the team is doing a damn good job. And they're being effective in managing these issues. No, probably not. Yeah. No, we're gonna have to move on to the next topic.

So, um, we're moving on to animal welfare, um, as a, a theme for the next section of this, um, hearing. And, uh, Rachel has a question. Thank you, chair. Um, minister, I'll jump straight into it. Why were the SBCA not included in consultation in the law that is currently going through the parliament?

When the fork industry were, obviously they were, uh, they were consulted in 2022 under labor, but they weren't under the specific, uh, legislation. And I understand they asked you on a number of occasions for updates on what was happening for, uh, PEG code. So why were they excluded from consultation with your office and with, uh, NPI?

Well, they only asked, uh, best of my memory. They only asked me once on that. Um, and that was only a couple of months ago in terms of why, I mean, they've, their point was their position was extremely clear. And, you know, we needed to work on what would work for the pork industry. These are the people that are gonna be affected the most.

They were the ones that are gonna have their livelihoods put at risk, um, by any changes. Those were the people I needed to talk about and talk to and work out, okay, what is a solution that not only improves welfare, but leaves behind a pork sector that is economically sustainable?

So have you read the article in the listener that, um, that quotes, um, any de from, uh, SA haven't, but also, um, scientists just regarding the summary science summary from NPI, which is basically dismissed by vets and by scientists and more welfare experts? Well, I mean, that science summary was, um, you know, there's studies in there that did the meta-analysis of a whole thousands of studies and showed that, you know, the key point here is you'll have a 14% higher relative risk of piglet mortality under a free farrowing system,

Which is actually, uh, disputed, um, by, uh, a recent study from the European Food Safety Agency. So that wasn't included in the summary. Some of the most recent, um, research, Well, you've still got thousands of pieces of research that were included. And I mean, I mean, I think, you know, the key thing here is there's this assumption that farmers wait on, um, instructions from government as to how to farm.

They basic, you know, if there's a smart way of doing things, farmers will adapt and they will do it. And they will look for ways that, um, you know, not only enhances the welfare of their animals, but also their survivability and profitability. Steve, stop on this. Who prepared that science summary?
Can you tell us that? Uh, I can't know who was the precise author of it, but John. John, yep.

Is that the answer, John? Yeah. John, obviously It's nice of you to bring him up. Yeah. We might get a chance to ask you some questions at the end of the week, I think, John. Yeah, yeah, absolutely. So I might, there was, there was a, there was a team that prepared it, but I was involved in that team. Right, absolutely. That's useful to know. Thank you. No worries. Um, Mr.

Minister, I've got, uh, an image from taken from a Ana Laki farm a few weeks ago. Yep. What's this Where, uh, this is a taranaki taranaki faring crate? I think so I'm happy pigs and faring crates. There's one dead piglet there underneath, which is another image of that if you wanna see it up close. We don't need to This would you agree, is not legal under our Animal welfare act to have, uh, mother pigs and cages like that based on the 2020 high court finding?

Well, no, the 2020 high court finding said that, um, that how the rule was put in place was done incorrectly, uh, or the process followed. And obviously that, uh, pigs been in a faring quite crate for 33 days, did not meet the purposes of the act, so not crate itself.

And so that's what we're looking at. How do we minimize that time in the crate to only what's necessary to save as many piglets as possible. Now, you're not gonna save every piglet, and I dare say po I would love to know how many more piglets died that night because someone broke into that property and scared all the pigs around. Uh, so why do you reject the advice of your National Animal Welfare Advisory Council that the best welfare outcomes for mother pigs and piglets is moving away from faring crates?

Well, no, I don't think they said that. Um, they, they did, no. Well, I mean, the analysis I had was, I mean, they could only look at the south in terms of that. Um, the advice I've got is, like I've said, 14% higher risk of mortality. Um, the key thing here is, you know, that we've gotta make sure that it's not just the sour, it's the piglets as well.

You've gotta balance everything up. It's not a perfect world in terms of farming. There's a number of things going wrong, uh, happening at the same time. And you've gotta come up with a smart balance. And that's what we've tried to do here, is achieve that balance between not only the sow's welfare, but also the piglet's welfare. One of the reasons you've articulated for the choice you've made to keep faring crates going Yeah.

Uh, the status quo for another 10 years, and then a change to the wiener and grower piglet cages. Yeah. But not a change in the size of the faring crates. A reduction in the length of time that sales must spend in those only after 10 years is the cost to farmers. But the advice to you was that a 350 South Farm would face a capital cost of approximately 507,000.

Yeah. For the option that you've chosen the legislation. And yet moving to the Naac option, which would be a removal of cell, of, um, fairing crates entirely would cost for a 350 cell farm, 595,000. So that's only a 17% difference. But it eliminates that problem of faring crate as part of our system. Why?

But it doesn't eliminate that ongoing for that. So it doesn't, um, doesn't remove that ongoing operational cost. And that is what kills you in a business. If you have to bake in a fact that you're gonna make a loss of a hundred thousand dollars a year every year going forward, that's the re that's the major cost.

Yes. Capital cost, you can get a loan, you can do all that, but that ongoing fact that your business is gonna be less profitable by that amount every year that I was, that was the key thing around actually. That's what adds on the extra cost to that Naac proposal. Have You prioritized that consideration over the consideration of animal welfare in your responsibility as the minister of animal welfare?

So in terms of the co the economics, that was the basis for the transition period in terms of the welfare that the, the issue that was why we went to minimize the length of time they could spend in the Crate to Max, and they would give us the best piglet survivability. So the welfare of both SOW and Piglet NPI recommended that you could get rid of mating cages by the 1st of July, 2026.

You've opted to give 10 years more for mating cages. Why have you chosen that when clearly it, there's no new infrastructure needed. It's simply a change in management practices, Uh, in terms of that. I mean, they, we looked at it as a whole package and said, we'll do this as one package.

Um, but I mean, that's something we can look at. Mm. So I've just got us up around the timeframe because, um, the pork industry would've been happy with a five year transition. Um, why 10 years? Why such a long time? Uh, to wait for what are minimal improvements in animal welfare? Basically because of the absolute mess we're getting at the moment around the, uh, resource management system.

Um, you are looking at farmers having to spend $40,000 or more to just to get a consent renewal is the feedback from the latest Federaled farmers survey. So all of us work, it's gonna require consent renewals for all of these properties. They're all gonna have to, you know, they're making major changes.

They're probably gonna be changing their effluent systems, which will likely result in a need to change their consent conditions. So we've got a program of work that will, you know, have this replacement in for the RMA, which will give a lot more certainty and reduce some of those costs that people are facing in terms of these consent renewals.

Now that's gonna take about estimate around four years to fully implement. So I wanted them to be able to not have to spend upwards of $200,000 or more that I'm seeing some farmers being faced with when they have to, to go through a notified consent process. I want them to be able to spend that on their business because if they're having to go through this process where they're getting all of those RMA costs, potentially on top of the physical costs they've gotta do, um, I'm sorry, we won't have a pork industry in this country. That's too much. But isn't it

Your to promote the animal welfare considering that your responsibility is the animal welfare Minister? And that's why, that's why we came up with the decisions we did around the length of time in the store. But in terms of transition, I think that's important, um, to have that timeframe for people to achieve those changes. Because if we don't have, um, a decent time for these people to make these changes, then quite frankly, you're gonna end up with mainly pork in this country being produced offshore at probably worse rates.

And I know, Steve, you've got a bill, Um, sues. Do you think that essentially farmers, including all pig farmers, are intrinsically aware of the health of their animals and that's vitally important to them? Uh, if the animal isn't healthy, they don't produce. Um, I mean it's, I've known that for a long time on a dairy farm, cow has to be healthy to make milk The end of review. Yep.

Mr. Chair? Um, yes, mark. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, minister, um, going back to gra pigs and the K values in the areas that were increased, do you think it's a fair, um, assertion that, that, that in some instances the advice you were getting didn't understand the social nature of especially grower pigs that often gravitate to an area in their store, which is often under a heat lamp, um, and that some of the investment proposals were inordinate expensive for a little benefit to the pigs?

Yeah. Well, what I've, my visits to pig farms, certainly what I've seen is you go in through the grower space and basically they're all sort of lying in a big rolling mold, uh, on top of each other in one corner of the pen. And the rest of the pen's effectively empty. Now, obviously, and I, I think a key point is that a lot of people miss is with this grower space, that the pigs will start with a growing space that is quite a high K value.

And it's, when they get to that point of 0.34, that's when they shift to the next, or at the moment it's 0.30. But that's when they get to that size, that's when they shift. Um, and so they might only be at that actual K value for a day or two before they shifted to an area with, um, to a new pen with more area for them to continue growing into.

Righto. Um, so we're getting way off, um, tack here 'cause we should be looking at the 24 25 financial. So, um, Steve's got a another question. Yeah. In terms of 24 25, a different line of questioning on live animal exports, um, you what work has actually been done on completing the commitment to reinstating live animal exports?

And why is the timeline blown out on that? Um, is it in the two hard basket and is it something your government is quietly kicking to touch? Uh, well, the, the work has been completed. It's in front of, um, cabinet in terms of, uh, those discussions.

I can't really go into When can we expect some, When I've got something to announce, I'll announce it, But it's still in train. It's still there. Yep. In June, you said that legislation would be ready soon, and we're nearly six months on from that point. So have you got an updated, uh, definition of soon When I've got an announcement to make, I'll make it. So Is it still soon?

Um, I can't really say, Is it because other coalition partners are starting to get cold? I can't talk about what happens in cabinet, you know that, Right. We're moving on. Keep going. Hang on. We've got to move on to food safety.

Right. So food safety, who is first off cab off the rank there, Got a question on food safety, if you want, You go, you go. Right. I'll Go. Hey, thank you for having me. Primary production, um, I'm interested in the assure quality meat inspections and have asked, uh, questions in the house.

And WP Q's minister, uh, you weren't Yeah, unfortunately today. Yeah, right. I did watch online. Yeah. Good. Yeah. Exciting. Um, and so probably these questions are similar to that, and one of them is the jobs loss where, or the potential for jobs loss at Assure Equality with the changes that you're consulting on.

So there's 650, um, trained assessors. What, what are you expecting to happen to their jobs? Well, I mean, that's all I'm up for consultation at the moment. Nothing's been decided, so I can't say whether there'll be job losses or not. Um, what I can say is that, uh, this model's used by 17% of the industry and, um, there hasn't been a issue at all with any meat coming from that part of the industry.

So on the, on the 17%, then the minister who responded to my questions, uh, I asked him, why, why has there not been any risk assessment done? No. And he said potentially that that 17% that you refer to was the risk assessment. So have, have you asked for any risk assessments or are you basing this all on?
